messages: Would 3D printing fit that mind-set, or is it more fiddly and gear-heavy than it seems for small-scale use?
I should mention I don't want activities that leave strong smells, like certain paints or resins—tidy and low-odor is preferred.
If there's an option that mixes light electronics and craft—something I can prototype with basic tools—that would check a lot of boxes for me.
I might have a friend or two join me occasionally, so if there's a hobby with easy short projects that work for 1–3 people, that's a plus.
Alright—based on what I've been dropping into the conversation up to now, could you put together a ranked shortlist of hobby options that would be a good fit?
For each hobby, include a brief explanation of why it aligns with my situation, the typical noises and odors involved, and whether it's easy to scale down for small-space use.
Also list the realistic starter costs (both minimal and a moderate upgrade path) and a monthly estimated spend if I were to take it up steadily.
Give a suggested first-month plan for each hobby: weekly time blocks, two small starter projects I could try in the evenings, and what I should avoid early on.
Indicate how friendly each hobby is to someone who prefers mostly solo practice but wants occasional group meetups.
Mention any accessibility/workaround tips for my physical limitations and thin-walled living situation without reiterating specifics.
If one option stands out as the best overall, highlight it and explain the trade-offs compared to two runner-up choices.
Also suggest one low-cost way to test each hobby in under a month—no large purchases, preferably borrowing, renting, or trial classes.
Finally, propose how I could budget time and money over the first three months to decide whether to keep going.
If you can, include links to community resources or types of local groups I should look for, but presented as categories rather than specific organizations.
If any of your top suggestions have significant drawbacks that would likely make me quit early, call those out bluntly so I can weigh the risk.
Thanks—after you give that, I might ask you to build a shopping and schedule checklist tailored to the one you pick as best.
